1:
The chapter opens by circling back in time, and the witnesses divide their labour between fixing the moment and reading the mercy. The grammatical-historical commentators insist the verb is a pluperfect. Adam Clarke would render it, ‘But Elisha had spoken unto the woman whose son he had restored,’ the whole notice recording an event ‘far past’ that belongs several years before the scene at court in v. 3. Charles Ellicott agrees the phrase ‘does not define the time,’ placing it after the raising of the Shunammite’s son and before the healing of Naaman, since the king still talks with Gehazi in v. 5; and he notes that the peculiar form of the pronoun marks the same hand that wrote 2 Kings 4, this famine being the very one foretold there (2 Kings 4:38). The famine, he adds, is one of Jehovah’s ‘four sore judgments’ (Ezekiel 14:21), and he doubts the ‘seven years’ need be pressed as a literal count.

The reformed John Gill keeps the pastoral thread taut: Elisha speaks to the woman whose son he had raised, one who ‘had reason to believe’ him, and directs her to sojourn abroad before the dearth strikes. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley hears in ‘the Lord hath called for a famine’ a whole doctrine of providence — the expression ‘intimates that all afflictions are sent by God, and come at his call or command’ — and reads the seven years as ‘a double time to the former famine under Elijah, which is but just, because they were still incorrigible under all the judgments of God, and the powerful ministry of Elisha.’

It is the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry who draws out the tenderness folded into the judgment. The kindness of the good Shunammite to the prophet is now ‘rewarded by the care that was taken of her in that famine’: she was not fed by miracle like the widow of Sarepta, but warned beforehand, so that she could remove ‘early, before the crowd,’ and take her stock with her unbroken. Henry marvels that the dearth was ‘peculiar to the land of Israel’ while neighbouring countries had plenty, a distinguishing stroke like the plagues of Egypt that showed ‘the immediate hand of God’ — for Israel’s sins, who professed relation to God, provoked him more than their neighbours’ (Amos 3:2). He lands the lesson gently: ‘It is our happiness to foresee an evil, and our wisdom, when we foresee it, to hide ourselves.’ The voices do not divide; they agree that the God who sends the famine goes before his friend with the warning, and that a cup of water given to a prophet is not forgotten in the day of drought.
2:
The verse is brief — the woman obeys, goes to the land of the Philistines, and sojourns seven years — and the witnesses read it as a small study in faith and prudence. The reformed John Gill makes obedience the point: she ‘did after the saying of the man of God,’ and she had every ground to believe him, being a woman ‘having a son given to her according to his word, and this restored to life, when dead, through his intercession.’ The word that raised her child could be trusted to guide her feet; past mercy is the seed of present faith.

The grammatical-historical commentators supply the geography that made the counsel wise. Charles Ellicott notes that ‘after the saying’ is more exactly ‘according to the word,’ and explains why the coastal plain was the place to flee: ‘the lowlands of the coast were not so subject to droughts as the limestone highlands of Israel’ — a fact he cross-checks against the patriarchs, who went down to Philistia or Egypt when the hill-country failed (Genesis 12:10; Genesis 26:1). The Philistines, besides, ‘dealt with foreign traders who put in to their shores’ (Joel 3:4-6), so that grain could be had where Israel had none. Keil and Delitzsch read the verse forward into the story it sets up: she ‘went with her household, and sojourned in the land of the Philistines seven years,’ and at the end of them will return to find her house and field seized by another, and so be driven to cry to the king.

Behind the bare travel-note the witnesses hear the same providence that shaped the first verse. The prophet’s word not only foretells the famine but appoints the refuge, and the refuge is exactly where nature and trade left a door open — the well-watered lowland of an old enemy’s country. There is a quiet irony the commentators let stand: Israel, the covenant land, is barren under judgment (Ezekiel 14:21), while Philistia, uncircumcised and idolatrous, has bread; the faithful woman must eat her exile’s loaf among strangers for seven long years. Yet she goes without recorded complaint, and the sequel will prove that the God who sent her out was keeping her portion for her against the day of her return. The voices converge on the plainest of lessons — that obedience to a sure word, even when it means leaving home for a heathen coast, is never finally the loser; the woman who once made a little chamber for the prophet is now herself lodged and provided for by the providence his word set in motion.
3:
At the seven years’ end the woman returns and goes out ‘to cry unto the king for her house and for her land,’ and the witnesses, though few and brief, agree on what has happened in her absence. The reformed John Gill reads the plain motive: she came back ‘either hearing that the famine was over, or believing that it was, the time being expired the prophet fixed for it.’ She reckons the seven years by the very word that sent her, and comes home on the strength of it.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott does the close work. He corrects the article — read simply ‘at seven years’ end’ — and traces her route, ‘from Shunem to Samaria,’ to the king’s seat. The cry is not for charity but for redress: ‘she found them in the possession of strangers.’ He weighs the two ways this could have come about: ‘the State may have occupied the property as abandoned by its owner; or, as is more likely, some neighbouring landowner may have encroached upon her rights’ while she was gone. Either way the law gave her a claim, and to the king — the fountain of justice — she therefore appeals. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley adds the domestic detail that makes the loss intelligible: her house and land, ‘having been forsaken by her, were possessed by her kindred’ — not distant robbers but her own relations, who filled the vacuum her flight had left.

The witnesses do not debate; they let the pathos of the verse speak. A woman once so settled that she needed nothing spoken for her to the king or the captain of the host now stands as a suppliant at the palace gate, dispossessed by the very neighbours or kinsfolk she had trusted. It is the reverse of her former estate, and it lays bare, as Gill and Ellicott together imply, how quickly the ground shifts under those who must leave what they have. Yet the return itself is an act of faith: she comes home the moment the appointed years expire, expecting that the God who foretold the famine has also numbered its ending, and that the King of Israel, for all his house’s idolatry, will still hear a wronged widow. The verse thus hangs suspended over its own resolution — the claimant is at the door, the hearing not yet granted — and the reader is made to feel, with the commentators, how much of a life can hinge on whether the powerful will do simple justice to the powerless. The next verses will show a providence already at work inside the throne-room before ever she opens her mouth.
4:
The king is found talking with Gehazi, asking him to ‘tell me, I pray thee, all the great things that Elisha hath done,’ and the witnesses seize on the timing and on a nagging difficulty. The reformed John Gill uses the scene as a chronological marker: this was ‘before he was dismissed from the service of the prophet, and consequently before the affair of Naaman’s cure, and so before the siege of Samaria’ — Gehazi is still ‘the servant of the man of God.’ Keil and Delitzsch read the moment for its dramatic providence: ‘just at that time the king was asking Gehazi to relate the great things Elisha had done,’ and was in the very act of recounting the restoration of the Shunammite’s son when its heroine walked in.

The knot is Gehazi’s leprosy. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke presses the objection hard: would a king ‘hold conversation with a leprous man,’ or trust the report of a servant ‘dismissed with the highest disgrace’ and branded for a liar by his disease? Clarke concludes it ‘agrees better with the chronology’ to place this after Naaman’s cure, the king’s ‘insatiable curiosity’ driving him to question the prophet’s own confidential servant, while keeping ‘a reasonable distance, as nothing but actual contact could defile.’ Against him the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sides with Keil: Gehazi ‘was not yet a leper,’ and in any case ‘lepers, though excluded from the city, were not excluded from conversation with others’ (Matthew 8:2; Luke 17:12), Naaman himself having been admitted to the palace. The way Gehazi is styled ‘the servant of the man of God’ seems to Ellicott to prove ‘the priority of the present narrative’ to the story of 2 Kings 5. John Wesley settles it simply: Gehazi was ‘formerly his servant,’ and ‘the law did not forbid conversing with lepers, but only dwelling with them.’

Ellicott closes with a historian’s appreciation, quoting Robertson Smith that this history ‘has a distinctly popular character… full of the dramatic touches and vivid presentations of detail’ of a story long told by mouth before it was written; the king who says to Gehazi, ‘Tell me all the great things that Elisha hath done,’ was surely not the only man who did. The real division among the witnesses is only whether the meeting fell before or after Naaman — a question of order, not of substance. On the substance they agree: God so times the woman’s petition that she arrives at the throne in the exact minute her own miracle is being told, so that her cause is won before she speaks. The verse is a lesson in the fine-tuning of providence, which orders even the small talk of kings to the rescue of the wronged.
5:
The stroke of coincidence lands: ‘as he was telling the king how he had restored a dead body to life… behold, the woman, whose son he had restored to life, cried to the king,’ and Gehazi cries out, ‘My lord, O king, this is the woman, and this is her son.’ The reformed John Gill identifies the case at once — the dead body was ‘the Shunammite’s son’ — while the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke names the wonder for what it is: ‘this was a very providential occurrence in behalf of the Shunammite.’ Gehazi’s narrative ‘was now corroborated by the woman herself; the king was duly affected, and gave immediate orders for the restoration of her land.’

The grammatical-historical commentators sharpen the picture. Charles Ellicott catches the vivid Hebrew tenses — ‘he (emphatic) was telling… and behold the woman was crying’ — so that the two actions overlap: ‘the woman came in, and began her prayer to the king, while he was talking with Gehazi about her and her son.’ He adds a homely realism, that the son, ‘now grown up, came as his mother’s escort,’ the living proof of the very miracle under discussion standing at her side. Keil and Delitzsch follow the sequel: the king, ‘so interested in her favour,’ after hearing her complaint ‘sent a chamberlain with her’ (a saris, as in 1 Kings 22:9), charged to procure not only her property but the produce of the land throughout her absence.

The witnesses do not divide; the verse is all convergence, and its lesson is the exquisite fitting of circumstance to need. Gehazi, of all people — the discredited servant, the man punished with Naaman’s leprosy — is made the herald whose word, confirmed in the flesh by mother and son, moves a heathenish king to do right. The very testimony that might have been doubted from a branded liar is authenticated the instant its subject walks through the door, so that, as the commentators imply, God forces the king to believe what he could otherwise have questioned. There is a quiet dignity in the risen son standing beside his pleading mother: the miracle is not a tale from the past but a young man before the throne, and the king cannot look at him without seeing that Elisha’s God is real and Elisha’s word is true. The witnesses agree that Heaven had arranged every actor and every minute of this scene — the king’s idle question, the servant’s story, the widow’s arrival — so that a wrong done in her absence should be righted in the one hour when the whole court was primed to grant it. Providence, they say, is never late.
6:
The king questions the woman, hears her story, and appoints an officer with the charge, ‘Restore all that was hers, and all the fruits of the field since the day she left the land, even until now.’ The witnesses read it as justice done in full measure. The reformed John Gill fills in what she told — how she had a son according to the prophet’s word when barren and her husband old, how the child sickened and died, and how Elisha’s prayers restored him — the whole marvel rehearsed a second time before the throne.

The grammatical-historical commentators press the terms of the grant. Charles Ellicott renders the officer, literally, a ‘eunuch’ (saris; compare Genesis 37:36; 1 Chronicles 28:1), and reads the ‘fruits’ as ‘revenues, produce in kind, which must have been paid out of the royal stores.’ From this he draws a shrewd inference: ‘this seems to imply that her land had been annexed to the royal domains’ — it was the crown, not a private neighbour, that had absorbed the abandoned estate, which is why the crown can repay both the field and every year’s harvest since. Keil and Delitzsch confirm the scope of the restitution: ‘all that was hers, and all the fruits of the field since the day that she left the land, even until now,’ a complete making-good of seven years’ loss.

The witnesses converge on the sweetness of the reversal. A woman who left as a refugee and returned as a dispossessed suppliant is sent home not merely reinstated but recompensed, the arrears of seven harvests counted back to her from the king’s own granaries. If Ellicott is right that the land had fallen to the crown, the king’s generosity is the more striking — he enriches himself by no calamity of his subject, but hands back estate and profit together. The reader is meant to see the long arc close: the hospitality once shown to Elisha in a walled-off upper room, the son given and the son raised, the seven years’ exile, the providential minute at court — all of it now issues in a royal decree that restores everything and more. The commentators leave the theology implicit but unmistakable: the God who is no man’s debtor keeps a long account, and repays the kindness done his servant with interest, using even an idolatrous king’s pen to write the widow’s deed of restitution. What she scattered in faith by leaving all at the prophet’s word she gathers again, pressed down and running over, in the day of her return.
7:
The scene shifts north to Damascus, where Elisha arrives as Ben-hadad lies sick and word runs, ‘The man of God is come hither.’ The witnesses divide first over why the prophet came. The reformed John Gill lists the guesses — to convert Gehazi (so the Jews), to confirm Naaman in the true religion, on account of the famine, or, most likely, ‘to anoint, or, however, to declare that Hazael would be king of Syria’ in fulfilment of the charge to Elijah (1 Kings 19:15). The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reviews the same theories — Rashi’s errand to fetch back Gehazi, Keil’s to anoint Hazael, Ewald’s retreat from the idolatrous Jehoram — and judges that ‘obviously all this rests upon pure conjecture’; the visit was plainly unexpected, Damascus and Samaria being at peace, so ‘we may fairly assume that a divine impulse led the prophet to Damascus,’ the revelation about Hazael coming to him only once he arrived.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry turns the sickbed into a sermon on affliction. ‘No honour, wealth, or power will secure men from the common diseases and disasters of human life; palaces and thrones lie as open to the arrests of sickness and death as the meanest cottage.’ He marvels that this heathen king, in his extremity, would ‘make Elisha his oracle’ — the same Ben-hadad who once sent a host to seize the prophet (2 Kings 6:14) now courts him as a father. ‘Affliction brings those to God who in their prosperity had made light of him,’ Henry writes, noting the sharp reproach that a king of Syria seeks the God of Israel while a king of Israel had sent to the god of Ekron (2 Kings 1:2): ‘God sometimes fetches to himself that honour from strangers which is denied him by his own professing people.’ And he reads the summons as proof that sickness ‘often gives men other thoughts of God’s ministers, and teaches them to value the counsels and prayers of those whom they had hated and despised.’

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke adds the rabbinic note that Elisha came ‘that he might lead Gehazi to repentance.’ Henry observes the honours heaped on the prophet — Ben-hadad sends to him rather than for him, by his prime minister Hazael, with a present of forty camels’ loading, addressing him as ‘thy son Ben-hadad,’ and enquiring of him as one ‘acquainted with the secrets of heaven.’ The witnesses hold the tension without resolving the mystery of the errand: they agree that whatever brought Elisha north, God brought him, and that a proud king’s sickbed became the unlikely theatre where a Gentile monarch bowed to the God of Israel and a servant of that God was honoured above the idols of Rimmon.
8:
Ben-hadad commissions Hazael: ‘Take a present in thine hand, and go, meet the man of God, and inquire of the Lord by him, saying, Shall I recover of this disease?’ The witnesses attend to the man, the gift, and the telling change in the king. The reformed John Gill notes only that Hazael was ‘the captain general of his army.’ The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott fills the portrait: Hazael ‘appears to have been the highest officer in Ben-hadad’s court,’ whom Josephus calls ‘the trustiest of his domestics.’ The present follows a settled custom when a seer is consulted (Numbers 22:7; 1 Samuel 9:7; 2 Kings 5:5), and ‘go to meet him’ does not mean Elisha was on the road but simply ‘go to the place where the prophet is to be found; seek an interview with him.’

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke is staggered by the scale of the gift — ‘what an immense present was this — forty camels’ burden of every good thing of Damascus!’ — wryly observing that ‘the prophet would need to have a very large establishment at Damascus to dispose of so much property.’ Keil and Delitzsch hover over the coming oracle and the famous textual knot of the next verses, where the written text (Chethib) reads ‘thou shalt not live’ and the margin (Keri) ‘thou shalt live,’ a crux the ancient versions and Masoretes resolve toward the Keri.

It is John Wesley who catches the deepest note in the errand. Ben-hadad, ‘in his health, bowed down in the house of Rimmon’ (compare 2 Kings 5:18), ‘but now he tends to enquire of the God of Israel.’ Here, says Wesley, is one more ‘instance of the change of men’s minds by affliction or sickness — that it often gives them other thoughts of God’s ministers, and teacheth them to value those whom they before hated and despised.’ The irony is not lost on the witnesses: the god of Damascus is silent in the sickroom, and the dying idolater sends past his own temple to the prophet of Jehovah. Yet the commentators let a shadow fall across the piety. The question is only ‘Shall I recover?’ — the anxiety of the body, not the soul — and the messenger dispatched to enquire of the God of truth is a man whose heart, Elisha will shortly read, is already bent on murder and a throne. The verse thus poises a real turning toward the true God against the false hearts through which even a sincere enquiry can pass, and sets the stage for one of the most searching interviews in the prophetic histories.
9:
Hazael goes with the present — ‘every good thing of Damascus, forty camels’ burden’ — and stands before Elisha with the deferential message, ‘Thy son Ben-hadad king of Syria hath sent me to thee, saying, Shall I recover of this disease?’ The reformed John Gill reads the gift as customary, ‘as was usual when a prophet or seer was consulted.’ The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott parses the abundance: there was a present ‘in money,’ and ‘in addition’ to it ‘every kind of good thing,’ for Damascus ‘was a great centre of traffic between Eastern and Western Asia’ (Ezekiel 27:18; Amos 3:12) — the city that gave its name to damask silk and to the famed Damascene sword-blades of mediaeval Europe. The forty camels, he insists, are ‘an actual train’: the Orientals ‘are fond of making the most of a gift in this way,’ and he quotes Chardin that ‘fifty persons often carry what a single one could very well carry.’

The address is the verse’s heart, and the wesleyan-arminian John Wesley seizes it: ‘Thy son’ — ‘he who before persecuted him as an enemy, now in his extremity honours him like a father.’ Ellicott confirms that ‘father’ was the respectful Oriental mode of addressing the prophet (compare 2 Kings 13:14; 2 Kings 6:21), so that the mighty king styles himself the seer’s son.

The witnesses let the reversal speak. The Syrian court that once marched armies to trap Elisha now empties its treasuries before him and bends its proudest officer to the errand of a suppliant. There is something almost comic, in Clarke’s earlier phrase, in the mountain of goods a prophet neither needed nor, it seems, kept — and the commentators recall that the same Elisha who accepted a widow’s hospitality had refused Naaman’s reward, so that the forty camels test nothing in him. The gift, rather, measures the desperation of the giver: a dying man will pour out a kingdom’s wealth for one word about his body. Yet the honour, however lavish, is skin-deep, offered by a king who feared the God of Israel only when his health failed and carried by a courtier whose deference cloaks a murderer’s design. The witnesses agree that the pageantry of the present throws into sharper relief the plainness of the answer Elisha is about to give — an answer no bribe could soften and no flattery could bend, spoken to a ‘son’ whose heart the prophet already reads to the bottom.
10:
Elisha’s answer is a riddle that has taxed every witness: ‘Go, say unto him, Thou mayest certainly recover: howbeit the Lord hath showed me that he shall surely die.’ The reformed John Gill untangles it by distinguishing the disease from the death: the sickness ‘was not a mortal one,’ so of it Ben-hadad would ‘certainly recover’; but he would ‘die a violent death,’ which the next clause predicts. Gill notes the textual variant — the written text reads ‘thou shalt not certainly recover’ — and lets both point the same way: the disease will not kill him, but another hand will.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott defends the received rendering against the alternative and rejects any charge that Elisha lied. He reads the words as ‘the tone of irony which he used to Gehazi’ (2 Kings 5:26): ‘Go, tell thy lord — as thou, the supple and unscrupulous courtier, wilt be sure to do — he will certainly recover. I know, however, that he will assuredly die, and by thy hand.’ Elisha, that is, does not command a lie but foretells the lie Hazael means to tell; the prophet ‘sees through Hazael’s character and designs.’ Ellicott spurns the reading that makes the prophet guilty of ‘complicity with Hazael,’ which ‘agrees neither with Elisha’s character nor with what follows.’

The wesleyan-arminian voices press the doctrine of contingency. Adam Clarke paraphrases: ‘God has not determined thy death, nor will it be a necessary consequence of the disease… but this wicked man will abuse the power and trust thou hast reposed in him, and take away thy life.’ Here, Clarke says, is ‘a farther proof of the doctrine of contingent events’ — Ben-hadad might have lived for all his sickness, yet will die because another wills it. John Wesley resolves the apparent contradiction the same way: ‘the first words contain an answer to Ben-hadad’s question, Shall I recover? — thou mayest, notwithstanding thy disease, which is not mortal’; the latter words are ‘the prophet’s addition — that he should die, not by the power of his disease, but by some other cause.’ The witnesses reach a settled agreement across their small disputes over the pointing: the oracle is no falsehood but a two-edged truth, and its sting is that the ‘other cause’ of the king’s death is standing in the room, holding the present, listening to the sentence he is about to execute. Elisha answers a question about a disease with a revelation about a man — and the man is Hazael.
11:
‘And he settled his countenance steadfastly, until he was ashamed: and the man of God wept.’ The verse is a knot of pronouns, and the witnesses work to untie who stares, who reddens, and why the prophet weeps. The reformed John Gill offers three possibilities — that Elisha ‘refrained himself’ from tears, or ‘turned his face… and covered it’ so Hazael could not see him weep, or, most likely, ‘set his face on Hazael, and looked at him so wistly’ — the prophet fixing the future murderer with a long, reading gaze.

The grammatical-historical commentators settle on that last sense. Keil and Delitzsch render it exactly: Elisha ‘made his face stand, and directed it upon Hazael’ — a fixed stare held ‘until Hazael was embarrassed,’ not, they insist, in any ‘shameless manner’ but until the man broke under it. Charles Ellicott agrees: Elisha ‘made his face stand, and set it upon Hazael,’ and ‘until he was ashamed’ means ‘until Hazael was disconcerted’ — ‘conscious that Elisha had read his thoughts aright,’ Hazael ‘shrank from that piercing gaze’ (compare 2 Kings 2:17). John Wesley reads it the same way — the prophet ‘fixed his eyes upon Hazael… till Hazael was ashamed, as apprehending the prophet discerned something of an evil and shameful nature in him.’

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke alone hesitates over the reference, asking ‘of whom does the author speak? Of Hazael, or of Elisha?’ He inclines to make the prophet the one who steels his face — a man who ‘had a murderer before him… endeavoured to conceal his feelings; at last his face reddened with anguish… and he burst out and wept’ — and notes a variant Septuagint text (the Complutensian) that turns the clause the other way, making Hazael ‘present gifts till he was ashamed.’ Whichever face is meant, the tears are unmistakably Elisha’s, and on their meaning the witnesses do not divide. The prophet weeps not for the dying Ben-hadad but for what he now sees written in the living Hazael — a grief born of foresight. The scene is one of the most affecting in the prophetic histories: a man of God and a man of war locked eye to eye, the one outfacing the other until guilt colours his cheek, and then the seer, who has read the whole bloody future in a single face, breaking down before the deeds he can foretell but not prevent. The more a man sees, the witnesses agree, the more he has to mourn.
12:
Hazael asks why his lord weeps, and Elisha answers with a catalogue of horrors: ‘Because I know the evil that thou wilt do unto the children of Israel: their strong holds wilt thou set on fire, and their young men wilt thou slay with the sword, and wilt dash their children, and rip up their women with child.’ The reformed John Gill sets the scene from Hazael’s side — he was ‘imagining it was for the death of Ben-hadad’ that the prophet wept, seeing no cause for grief in a master’s passing that would raise himself.

The grammatical-historical commentators anchor the prophecy in its fulfilment. Keil and Delitzsch observe that this ‘cruel conduct towards Israel’ only elaborates the brief word the Lord gave Elijah about Hazael (1 Kings 19:17), and that its accomplishment is recorded (2 Kings 10:32-33; 2 Kings 13:3) and echoed by the prophets (Hosea 10:14; Hosea 14:1). Adam Clarke and Charles Ellicott point to the same later chapters (2 Kings 13:7), Ellicott noting soberly that ‘the cruelties enumerated here were the ordinary concomitants of warfare in that age’ (Amos 1:13; Hosea 13:16; 2 Kings 15:16) — not a monstrous exception but the routine savagery of ancient war, foreseen in advance.

The witnesses read the exchange for its terrible pathos. Elisha weeps over deeds not yet done, wrung by a foreknowledge that is pure grief and no gladness — for though the sins of Israel had earned this scourge, the prophet ‘did not desire the woeful day.’ The catalogue is deliberately unsparing: fortress and fire, the young men cut down, the infants dashed, the very unborn ripped from the womb — the whole compass of a conquest that spares neither strength nor innocence. And the man who will do it stands listening, still protesting his innocence in the next breath. The commentators let the horror instruct: that God should reveal such things to his servant is a mercy of warning to Israel and a testimony to the truth of prophecy, yet it lays on the seer a burden of sorrow the murderer cannot feel. Hazael sees only a weeping old man; Elisha sees burning towns and childless mothers. The verse thus binds two truths the witnesses hold together — that the future is naked before God, and that to know it is to weep. What is prophecy to the prophet is, for the moment, only a riddle to the ambitious courtier, who will read its meaning soon enough in his own hands.
13:
Hazael recoils — ‘But what, is thy servant a dog, that he should do this great thing?’ — and Elisha answers, ‘The Lord hath showed me that thou shalt be king over Syria.’ The witnesses divide sharply over the tone of Hazael’s protest. The reformed John Gill already hesitates between two senses of ‘dog’: either ‘a vile, impudent, fierce, and cruel creature’ shrinking from the barbarity, or ‘a mean, abject creature… incapable of doing such great things’ — and he leans, given the word ‘great,’ toward the latter.

The grammatical-historical commentators read hypocrisy. Keil and Delitzsch call it ‘feigned humility’ — ‘the dog’ meaning ‘so base a fellow’ (1 Samuel 24:14) — and Charles Ellicott agrees that Hazael answers ‘in a tone of pretended amazement and self-depreciation,’ his ‘exaggerated humility… betraying the hypocrite.’ Elisha’s reply, ‘Jehovah hath made me see thee king,’ strips the disguise; and Ellicott cites Bähr that, ‘startled by the revelation of his secret plans, Hazael turned away without answering.’

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke is the bold dissenter and champions a wholly different reading. The common interpretation, he says, is ‘wrongly’ taken: Hazael is not struck with horror but ‘delighted… at the prospect,’ his question expressing not revulsion but the surprise of an insignificant man at the vista of power. ‘A dog bears not, in Scripture, the character of a cruel, but of a despicable animal’ — so the sense is, ‘Can such a poor, worthless fellow, such a dead dog, perform such mighty actions?’ The prophet’s reply, promising him the throne, ‘is plainly calculated to satisfy the astonishment he had excited,’ and, quoting Patten, Clarke insists Hazael’s cry ‘has no tincture’ of horror — ‘a dog… is a despicable animal; nor does he who is shocked with its barbarity call it a Great deed.’ The immediate murder that follows proves, for Clarke, that there was no revulsion to overcome.

So the debate is genuine and pointed: does Hazael shudder at the crime or thrill at the crown? Gill, Keil, and Ellicott hear self-serving humility or a hypocrite’s modesty; Clarke hears naked ambition mistaking itself for lowliness. Yet the witnesses converge on the moral. Hazael has ‘a good opinion of himself, how much better than he deserved,’ supposing it impossible he should ever do such things — and the sequel exposes the self-flattery. It is possible, they warn, ‘for a wicked man… to express great abhorrence of a sin, and yet afterwards to be reconciled to it’; we ‘are apt to think ourselves sufficiently armed against those sins which yet we are afterwards overcome by.’ The throne Elisha names will not create Hazael’s cruelty but only give it scope; power will simply unlock what the heart already holds.
14:
Hazael leaves the prophet, returns to his master, and to the question ‘What said Elisha to thee?’ answers, ‘He told me that thou shouldest surely recover.’ The witnesses fasten on the half-truth. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes precisely what Hazael does and does not repeat: he gives back ‘Elisha’s actual words — Thou wilt certainly live — but not the tone and gesture which accompanied them,’ suppressing the weeping stare and the sentence of death that followed. That Hazael’s report carries no negative particle is itself, Ellicott has argued, evidence for the received reading of v. 10.

The reformed John Gill marks only that Hazael ‘came to his master, Ben-hadad king of Syria,’ but the fuller indictment comes through the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, who dwells on the treachery of the message. Hazael, he says, ‘basely cheated his master, and belied the prophet’: Elisha had told him the king should die, ‘but he unfairly and unfaithfully concealed that,’ whether ‘because he was loth to put the king out of humour with bad news, or because hereby he might the more effectually carry on that bloody design which he conceived when he was told he should be his successor.’ Henry finds in it the devil’s own strategy: ‘the devil ruins men by telling them they shall certainly recover and do well, so rocking them asleep in security, than which nothing is more fatal.’ The lie was ‘an injury to the king, who lost the benefit of this warning to prepare for death, and an injury to Elisha, who would be counted a false prophet.’

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch simply carry the narrative forward — the flattering report given, the deed will follow on the morrow. The witnesses do not divide; they agree that the verse is a study in how a soft lie prepares a hard crime. Hazael tells his king the very words most welcome to a sick man’s ear, and by that mercy-shaped falsehood robs him of the one thing the true oracle offered — time to set his house in order before the end. The flattery that soothes is the flattery that kills. And the prophet who spoke plainly is left, for a day, with his word apparently falsified in the king’s hearing, until the morning proves which of the two men told the truth. The commentators let the lesson stand for every age: those who whisper ‘thou shalt surely recover’ over the dying, and over the spiritually perishing, may be doing the enemy’s work in a friend’s voice.
15:
‘On the morrow… he took a thick cloth, and dipped it in water, and spread it on his face, so that he died: and Hazael reigned in his stead.’ The reformed John Gill hears the haste of ambition: it was done on the very next day, ‘in such haste was Hazael to be king, as the prophet said he would be.’ The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke gives the mechanics — the ‘thick cloth’ is understood by the versions as ‘a hairy or woollen cloth,’ and the king was ‘smothered, or suffocated’ — and John Wesley adds that it was ‘spread so closely that he choked him therewith.’

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott does the philology and the apologetics together. He settles that ‘he took’ means Hazael, ‘the nearest subject,’ and defends the placing of his name at the verse’s end as the natural way to announce a new king. The cloth, the makber, is a word found nowhere else but cognate to one in 1 Samuel 19:13, meaning ‘something plaited or interwoven’ — a quilt or coverlet which, soaked and laid on the face, would ‘prevent respiration.’ He records Josephus’s guess of a mosquito net and the old commentator Clericus’s shrewd note on Hazael’s motive — to suffocate the man quietly, ‘ne vi interemptus videretur,’ that he might not seem slain by violence. Ellicott then repels a modern slander with vigour: when Duncker ventures that ‘Elisha incited Hazael to the murder,’ he ‘simply betrays an utter incapacity for understanding… Hebrew prophecy’; the writer of Kings ‘did not intend to represent Elisha as a deceiver of foreign sovereigns and a traitor to his own.’ And he crowns the note with archaeology: on the Black Obelisk of Shalmaneser II, now in the British Museum, ‘Haza’ilu of the land of Damascus’ is named among the conquered — the murderer’s own reign inscribed in stone.

The witnesses converge on the grim fulfilment. Every strand of Elisha’s interview now ties off: the king who would ‘surely die’ dies, not of his disease but under a wet cloth; the courtier who protested he was but a dog seizes the throne within a day; the tears the prophet shed over Israel’s future find their first warrant in a pillow of murder. The commentators are careful, with Ellicott, to keep the prophet clean of the crime — foretelling is not abetting — and to keep the guilt where it belongs, on the hand that spread the cloth. Prophecy has named the man and the throne; ambition has supplied the means; and a great monarch, terror of the mighty in his day, goes down to the pit stifled by his most trusted servant. So swiftly, the witnesses observe, does a heart that thought itself incapable prove capable, once the crown is within reach.
16:
The narrative turns south to Judah with a dense synchronism, and the witnesses split their work between a textual crux and a portrait of a bad son of a good father. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott and the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke both stumble on the clause ‘Jehoshaphat being then king of Judah.’ Clarke shows the arithmetic will not stand: Jehoshaphat reigned twenty-five years and Jehoram his son but eight (1 Kings 22:42; 2 Kings 8:17), so the words ‘greatly disturb the chronology’ and are ‘wanting’ in many manuscripts and versions; Ellicott likewise calls the clause ‘a spurious anticipation’ that ‘should be omitted,’ finding ‘no trace elsewhere of a co-regency of Jehoram with his father.’

Against them stand the voices who take the clause to signal a real second co-regency. The reformed John Gill begins the count from Joram of Israel’s reign, which opened in Jehoshaphat’s eighteenth year. John Wesley reconstructs the history: Jehoram was ‘first made king or viceroy’ earlier, at the Ramoth-gilead expedition, that office ending at his father’s return; ‘but now Jehoshaphat, being not far from his death, and… fearing some competition among’ his sons, ‘makes Jehoram king the second time, as David did Solomon upon the like occasion.’ Keil and Delitzsch read it the same way — the father handed over the government two years before his death.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry leaves the numbers to draw the character, and it is a warning to godly homes. ‘Parents cannot give grace to their children’; many godly parents have had ‘the grief and shame’ of wicked offspring, and ‘if the children of good parents prove wicked, commonly they are worse than others’ (compare Luke 11:26). Henry traces the ruin to two errors of Jehoshaphat’s: he ‘did ill to marry him to the daughter of Ahab,’ for ‘no good could come of an alliance with an idolatrous family’ and ‘those that are ill-matched are already half-ruined’; and he ‘did not do well to make him king in his own life-time,’ gratifying a pride ‘pernicious to young people’ and bringing ‘a curse upon his family, as Eli did.’ Jehoram ‘chose the house of Ahab for his pattern rather than his father’s house, and this choice was his ruin.’ The witnesses thus hold two things at once: a knot of chronology they resolve either by emending the text (Clarke, Ellicott) or by a genuine co-regency (Wesley, Henry, Keil), and a moral they all confess — that a nation may be ‘justly punished with the miseries of a bad reign for not improving the blessings of a good one,’ and that grace runs in no man’s blood.
17:
‘Thirty and two years old was he when he began to reign; and he reigned eight years in Jerusalem.’ The reformed John Gill reads the accession as the second one, ‘in the lifetime of his father,’ keeping to his co-regency scheme. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke divides the eight years — ‘he reigned three years with Jehoshaphat his father, and five years alone’ — and dates them by Archbishop Ussher from the world-year 3112 to 3119.

The grammatical-historical commentators supply the substance the bare summary omits. Keil and Delitzsch gather the whole reign into a sentence: Joram ‘had married a daughter of Ahab, namely Athaliah’ (2 Kings 8:26), ‘and walked in the ways of the house of Ahab, transplanting the worship of Baal into his kingdom.’ Worse, ‘immediately after the death of Jehoshaphat he murdered his brothers, apparently with no other object than to obtain possession of the treasures which his father had left them’ (2 Chronicles 21:2-4). Such wickedness ‘would have been followed by the destruction of Judah, had not the Lord preserved a shoot to the royal house for David’s sake’ — the ‘light’ or lamp promised in 1 Kings 11:36, which Keil reads as kept alive by keeping his sons upon the throne, the theme v. 19 will state outright. Charles Ellicott stands back to feel the change of register: ‘How different are these short annalistic summaries, the work of the Judaean compiler, from the rich and flowing narratives about Elijah and Elisha!’

The witnesses do not debate; they let the terse formula frame a life. A man comes to the throne in the prime of his strength, thirty-two years old, with every advantage of a pious father’s training and a settled kingdom, and squanders it in eight short years upon Baal and blood. Ellicott’s note on style carries a quiet theology: the historian who lingered lovingly over the prophets hurries past this king, and the brevity is itself a verdict — there is little to tell of a reign that added nothing to the kingdom of God but idolatry and fratricide. The commentators thus prepare the reader for the chastisements to come. The eight years are counted out like a short lease on a wasted estate; and behind the dry regnal figures stands the one bright fact Keil insists on — that the throne survives this occupant at all only because God is keeping a promise older than the man who sits on it, holding the lamp of David steady in a hand that would gladly have let it fall.
18:
‘And he walked in the way of the kings of Israel, as did the house of Ahab: for the daughter of Ahab was his wife: and he did evil in the sight of the Lord.’ The witnesses lay the whole blame at the door of one marriage. The reformed John Gill reads the plain charge — he ‘imitated them in idolatry’ — and the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke names the wife: ‘this was the infamous Athaliah; and through this marriage Jehoshaphat and Ahab were confederates,’ a friendship ‘continued after Ahab’s death.’

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott traces the poison to its Tyrian source. As ‘son-in-law of Ahab and Jezebel,’ Jehoram ‘lent his countenance to the cultus of the Tyrian Baal,’ and ‘under the influence of his wife Athaliah… slew his six brothers directly after his accession’ (2 Chronicles 21:4). He preserves the arresting observation of Michaelis, that this very reign coincides with ‘the building of Carthage… Dido, her husband Sichaeus, her brother Pygmalion, king of Tyre’: ‘by marriage Tyre brought its then prevalent spirit, and a vast amount of evil, into the two Israelitish kingdoms.’ The chronicler’s fuller details are here passed over, Ellicott notes, from ‘the studied brevity of the compiler in the case of less important characters.’

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley lays the theme bare, and his words become the verse’s motto. The unequal marriage, ‘though Jehoshaphat possibly designed it as a means of uniting the two kingdoms under one head, is here and elsewhere noted as the cause both of the great wickedness of his posterity, and of those sore calamities which befell them. No good could be reasonably expected from such an union. Those that are ill matched are already half-ruined.’ The witnesses do not divide; they read the verse as a single, sobering chain of cause and effect. A well-meant dynastic alliance yoked David’s line to Jezebel’s, planted the Tyrian Baal in Jerusalem, armed a daughter of that house to make her husband a fratricide, and set loose upon Judah the very spirit that had ruined the north. The commentators see in it the fruit of a policy that weighed politics above piety: Jehoshaphat, a good king, sought union and reaped corruption, and his son ‘did evil in the sight of the Lord’ not by his own invention only but by the character he married. The lesson they draw reaches past the throne to every hearth — that the choice of a yoke-fellow can decide the destiny of a house, and that an alliance which slights the fear of God carries its judgment folded up inside it.
19:
‘Yet the Lord would not destroy Judah for David his servant’s sake, as he promised him to give him alway a light, and to his children.’ The witnesses read the verse as the covenant standing firm over a rotten reign. The reformed John Gill guards the ground of the mercy: it was ‘not for his merits, but for the mercy he assured him of’ — sheer promise, not desert. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott traces the ‘light’ to its roots in the Davidic covenant (1 Kings 15:4; 1 Kings 11:36; and the great promise of 2 Samuel 7:12-16), and weighs the disputed clause ‘and to his children’: Keil would render ‘to give him, that is, his sons, a lamp,’ making the phrase an explanation — the light kept burning by keeping David’s descendants on the throne.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch insist the mercy did not cancel the chastisement: ‘the divine chastisement was not omitted,’ the ungodliness of Joram being punished ‘partly by the revolt of the Edomites and of the city of Libnah’ and ‘partly by a horrible sickness of which he died’ (2 Chronicles 21:12-15). Adam Clarke glosses the promise simply — ‘to give him a successor in his own family.’

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley lifts the ‘alway’ to its horizon. The succession was to hold ‘until the coming of the Messiah: for so long, and not longer, this succession might seem necessary for the making good of God’s promise and covenant made with David.’ Once the Messiah came, ‘there was no more need of any succession, and the sceptre might… depart from Judah… because the Messiah was to hold the kingdom forever in his own person.’ The witnesses converge on the deepest note in the chapter. God will not destroy Judah, though justice might; and the reason lies wholly outside Joram — in an oath sworn to David and aimed at David’s greater Son. The lamp is not the merit of the man on the throne but the faithfulness of the God who keeps the line unbroken until it flowers in Christ. So the commentators hold two truths in one hand: that this generation is scourged for its sin, Edom and Libnah torn away and the king rotting toward a foul death; and that the dynasty survives every apostasy because a covenant older than any of its failures cannot fail. Judgment falls on the wicked king; the promise runs on to the Messiah; and the single verse teaches that the church, and the world’s salvation, hang not on the goodness of kings but on the steadfastness of God.
20:
‘In his days Edom revolted from under the hand of Judah, and made a king over themselves.’ The witnesses read the first of Joram’s chastisements. The reformed John Gill measures the loss historically: Edom ‘had been tributary to Judah ever since the times of David, for the space of one hundred and fifty years.’ The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott makes plain the connection of thought: ‘although Jehovah was not willing to extirpate Judah, yet He suffered it to be seriously weakened by the defections’ that follow — the revolt is mercy and judgment braided together. He records Josephus’s account that the Edomites ‘slew the vassal king appointed over them’ by Jehoshaphat (1 Kings 22:47), Edom having been ‘subject to the hegemony of Judah from the time of the disruption under Rehoboam.’

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry sets the revolt in the long light of prophecy. The Edomites, he observes, ‘were, after this, bitter enemies to the Jews, as appears by the prophecy of Obadiah’ and by the cry of Psalms 137:7. And he hears in it the working-out of an ancient word: ‘now Isaac’s prophecy was fulfilled, that this Esau the elder should serve Jacob the younger; yet, in process of time, he should break that yoke from off his neck’ (Genesis 27:40). What had been foretold at the patriarch’s bedside comes to pass in the reign of a wicked king: the yoke Jacob’s house had laid on Esau’s for a hundred and fifty years is at last shaken off.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse’s brevity only sharpens its point. The revolt of Edom is no mere political reverse but a stroke of the very providence that spared Judah for David’s sake. God will keep his covenant with the dynasty, but he will not shield this king from loss; the promise of the lamp and the discipline of the rod fall in the same reign. There is a further irony the commentators let stand: a house that had forsaken the God of its fathers finds the subjects of its fathers forsaking it, and the ancient oracle over Esau and Jacob turns, in Joram’s day, from blessing to rebuke. The witnesses thus knit this small annalistic notice into the chapter’s theology — that empire and tribute are held only by God’s leave, that sin loosens what grace once secured, and that the long memory of Scripture reaches from Isaac’s tent to Edom’s revolt, binding a single verse of Kings to the whole story of the two brothers and the yoke between them.
21:
‘So Joram went over to Zair, and all the chariots with him: and he rose by night, and smote the Edomites which compassed him about… and the people fled into their tents.’ The witnesses first fix the place. The reformed John Gill takes Zair for ‘a city in Edom… some take it to be the same with Seir.’ The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke agrees Zair ‘is the same as Seir, a chief city of Idumea,’ and reads the action as a desperate breakout: ‘the Israelites were surrounded by the Idumeans; and… in the night Joram and his men cut their way through them, and so got every man to his tent,’ unable to press the advantage, so that ‘Edom revolted… unto this day.’

The grammatical-historical commentators confess the passage’s obscurity. Keil and Delitzsch call the account ‘very obscure,’ picturing Joram rising by night to smite the Edomites ‘round about’ and their chariot-captains. Charles Ellicott allows ‘there may be a lacuna of a few lines in the text here, or the compiler, in his desire to be brief, may have become obscure,’ and reconstructs the sense: Jehoram was ‘hemmed in by the Edomites in the mountains, and… attempted escape under cover of night,’ cutting his way through their ranks and their chariotry, while ‘the army of Jehoram was glad to escape from the scene of its ill success.’ The name Zair he takes for a variant of Seir. He adds a grim conjecture from Joel 3:19 — ‘Edom shall be a desolate wilderness for the violence against the children of Judah’ — that at their revolt the Edomites ‘massacred the Jews who had settled in the country,’ the old enmity of Genesis 27:40 breaking out afresh.

The witnesses reach a shared reading of a hollow victory. Joram wins the night skirmish and loses the war: he breaks the encirclement but cannot break the revolt, and his troops scatter homeward, glad merely to have escaped. The chariots that went ‘over to Zair’ return with nothing gained but survival. Behind the tangled Hebrew the commentators discern a settled truth — that God had decreed Edom’s independence as a judgment, and no feat of arms could undo what heaven had loosed. A king may cut his way through a besieging host by night and yet find in the morning that the thing he came to recover is gone for good. The obscurity of the text, the commentators imply, only mirrors the murk of the campaign itself: a confused midnight fight ending in a retreat dressed up as a rescue, and a rebellion that stands ‘unto this day.’ Judah’s grip on Edom, held since David, is broken in the dark, and not all Joram’s chariots can close the hand again.
22:
‘Yet Edom revolted from under the hand of Judah unto this day. Then Libnah revolted at the same time.’ The witnesses read the settling of the loss and the spread of the contagion. The reformed John Gill supplies the military reason the revolt held: Joram was ‘not pursuing the enemy, and taking the advantage of the victory, but returning to his own land’ — the night breakout of the previous verse won nothing lasting because it was not followed up.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott dates the phrase and confirms it from the monuments. ‘Unto this day’ means ‘down to the time of composition of the original account,’ and the notice ‘is borne out by the Assyrian monuments’: Esarhaddon and Assurbanipal name ‘Qa’us-gabri king of Udumu’ — Edom — ‘among their tributaries,’ an independent kingdom in later Assyrian records. The revolt of Libnah he reads as contagion: ‘the success of Edom encouraged Libnah to throw off the Judaean supremacy’ (a town of the lowland, Joshua 10:29; Joshua 15:42; Joshua 21:13), and Keil links it with the Philistine invasion of 2 Chronicles 21:16, Libnah returning to Judah only from Hezekiah’s day (2 Kings 19:8).

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gives the revolt of Libnah its moral edge, reading it as a protest of conscience. Libnah was ‘a city in Judah, in the heart of his country, a priests’ city; the inhabitants of this city shook off his government because he had forsaken God, and would have compelled them to do so too’ (compare 2 Chronicles 21:11); ‘in order that they might preserve their religion they set up for a free state.’ The witnesses do not divide; together they show the kingdom fraying at both edges at once — a subject nation breaking away abroad, a priestly town seceding at home. The deeper cause, they agree, is the same in both: Joram ‘had forsaken God,’ and a throne that abandons heaven loses its hold on earth. Ellicott’s archaeology and Henry’s theology meet in a single verdict — the revolt was real, lasting, and inscribed in the stones of Nineveh, and it was also righteous, the recoil of men who would not be driven into idolatry by a king who had sold his own soul. So the chapter’s pattern deepens: the God who keeps David’s lamp burning still lets the wicked son feel his kingdom shrink in his hands, until even a city of priests would rather be free than serve an apostate crown.
23:
‘And the rest of the acts of Joram, and all that he did, are they not written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Judah?’ The verse is the compiler’s standing formula of closure, and the witnesses use it to point the reader beyond the terse annals to the fuller record. The reformed John Gill clarifies the reference: the book meant is ‘not the canonical book of Chronicles… but the annals of the kings of Judah, written by persons appointed for that purpose’ — the state records from which the sacred historian drew, now lost. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott both send us to the inspired parallel that survives: ‘several remarkable particulars relative to Joram may be found in’ the twenty-first chapter of Chronicles, Ellicott directing us especially to 2 Chronicles 21:11-19 — the letter from Elijah, the Philistine and Arabian invasion, and the loathsome disease of which the king died.

The formula itself repays attention. It appears over king after king in the book of Kings, a refrain that measures each reign against the same yardstick and files it away with the same question, ‘are they not written?’ The witnesses note that the historian is not writing biography but a theological history: he keeps what serves his purpose — the idolatry, the revolts, the mercy shown for David’s sake — and leaves the ‘rest of the acts’ to the civil chronicle. What the compiler passes over in his ‘studied brevity’ the Chronicler preserves, so that the two records together give the full measure of the man: a king who murdered his brothers, seduced Judah into the harlotries of Ahab’s house, received a prophet’s written warning, and was struck at last with the disease that carried out his bowels, dying, as the parallel records, ‘without being desired’ (2 Chronicles 21:19).

The witnesses draw the quiet lesson that lies in such a closing verse. Every reign is ‘written’ — if not fully in the pages men keep, then wholly in the book God keeps — and the very reticence of Scripture is a kind of judgment. There was much a court annalist might have chronicled of Joram; the Spirit records chiefly his sin and his sorrows, that the reader might learn from a wasted reign what the man himself never did. The formula that seems merest bookkeeping thus carries the chapter’s theme to its sober end: the acts of kings are all on file, the good remembered and the evil not forgotten, and a life that added nothing to the kingdom of God is summed up and set aside in a single referring sentence, its fuller tale left to a chronicle now lost and to the God who forgets nothing.
24:
‘And Joram slept with his fathers, and was buried with his fathers in the city of David: and Ahaziah his son reigned in his stead.’ The reformed John Gill reads the sleep plainly — he ‘died as they did’ — but the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott catches the sting the customary phrase half-conceals: Joram was buried in the city of David, ‘but not in the royal tombs’ (2 Chronicles 21:20). The formula of honour is granted with one hand and withdrawn with the other; a king who lived like Ahab is denied a place among the kings he disgraced.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch turn at once to the successor and to a famous number. Ahaziah, ‘the youngest son of Joram, ascended the throne in the twenty-second year of his age’; the reading in the parallel that makes him forty-two ‘rests upon a copyist’s error, namely, a confusion of’ the letters for twenty and forty (2 Chronicles 22:2). Since his father died at forty, a son of forty-two would be older than his own father; Keil therefore reckons Ahaziah born in his father’s nineteenth year, and explains that older brothers were possible because ‘early marriages are common in the East’ and the princes had concubines besides their first wife (2 Chronicles 21:17).

The witnesses do not divide; they let the burial and the succession together pronounce the reign’s verdict. Joram passes to his grave with the outward decencies of a king — ‘slept with his fathers,’ laid in David’s city — yet shut out of the sepulchre of the kings, a dishonour the Chronicler makes explicit and Ellicott will not let the reader miss. Even the grammar of honour, the commentators imply, can carry a rebuke: the stock phrase is kept, but the reality behind it is withheld. And the throne passes not to reform but to more of the same, for Ahaziah is ‘the youngest son’ of a fratricide and the grandson of Ahab through his mother, so that the poison is handed on undiluted. The numerical crux Keil resolves — twenty-two, not forty-two — keeps the generations in order and guards the text’s truth against a scribe’s slip; but the deeper continuity it preserves is a mournful one. A bad king is buried in dishonour, and his youngest son takes up the crown and the idolatry together, so that the discipline of God upon this house is not yet done, and the next reign, the witnesses know, will be shorter and darker still.
25:
‘In the twelfth year of Joram the son of Ahab king of Israel did Ahaziah the son of Jehoram king of Judah begin to reign.’ The reformed John Gill notes only that this king ‘is called Jehoahaz, and said to be the youngest son of Jehoram’ — a name reversed, Ahaziah and Jehoahaz being the same elements in a different order.

The verse belongs chiefly to the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, who makes it an essay on ‘little kings.’ As among common men ‘there are some that we call little men, who make no figure,’ so ‘among kings there are some whom… we may call little kings,’ and this Ahaziah is one: he ‘looks mean in the history, and in God’s account vile, because wicked.’ Henry reads a bad omen in the very names of the two royal houses. ‘It is too plain an evidence of the affinity between Jehoshaphat and Ahab that they had the same names in their families at the same time’ — Ahab had an Ahaziah and a Jehoram, and so, in echoing succession, did Judah. ‘Names indeed do not make natures,’ he grants, and it was ‘a bad omen to Jehoshaphat’s family to borrow names from Ahab’s,’ for they borrowed the names without ‘the devotion of their significations’ — Ahaziah, ‘taking hold of the Lord,’ and Jehoram, ‘the Lord exalted.’

Henry then reads Ahaziah’s whole story out of his family ties. His ‘relation to Ahab’s family was the occasion’ both of his wickedness — he ‘walked in the way of the house of Ahab’ (v. 27), his mother being Ahab’s daughter, so that he ‘sucked in wickedness with his milk’ — and of his fall, for Joram his uncle drew him into the fatal expedition to Ramoth-gilead, where the king of Israel was wounded (v. 28) and where Ahaziah’s own visit would soon cost him his life. ‘Providence so ordered it,’ Henry writes, ‘that he who had been debauched by the house of Ahab might be cut off with them, when the measure of their iniquity was full.’ The witnesses do not divide; they take the verse’s dry date and draw from it the chapter’s closing warning. ‘When men choose wives for themselves they must remember they are choosing mothers for their children.’ The affinity Jehoshaphat forged with Ahab in a marriage now bears its last bitter fruit in a grandson who reigns but a year and dies in the ruin of the house he loved: ‘those who partake with sinners in their sins must expect to partake with them in their plagues.’
26:
‘Two and twenty years old was Ahaziah when he began to reign; and he reigned one year in Jerusalem. And his mother’s name was Athaliah, the daughter of Omri king of Israel.’ The witnesses wrestle with a famous numerical difficulty and mark the ominous shadow of his mother. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch state the crux and its solution: the parallel’s ‘forty-two’ (2 Chronicles 22:2) ‘rests upon a copyist’s error, namely, a confusion of’ the Hebrew letters for twenty and forty — for a son of forty-two would be two years older than his own father.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke lays the whole difficulty bare and canvasses the ingenious way out. He rehearses the ‘heavy difficulty’ — that the Chronicles reading makes ‘the son two years older than his own father’ — and reports John Lightfoot’s harmonizing solution, that ‘Ahaziah was the son of two and forty years; namely, of the house of Omri, of whose seed he was by the mother’s side,’ the forty-two counting back ‘to the second of Omri.’ But Clarke will not rest there. ‘After all, here is a most manifest contradiction, that cannot be removed but by having recourse to violent modes of solution’; he is ‘satisfied the reading in’ Chronicles ‘is a mistake,’ that we should read twenty-two in both places, and with Calmet he asks which is safer — to own that ‘transcribers have made some mistakes in copying the sacred books,’ or to allege real contradictions and then patch them with solutions that satisfy no ‘unprejudiced mind.’

The reformed John Gill simply refers the reader to his solution of the same knot, while the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott turns from the number to the mother. Athaliah is called ‘the daughter of Omri’ though strictly his granddaughter, ‘Omri being mentioned rather than Ahab as the founder of the dynasty, and the notorious example of its wickedness’ (compare Micah 6:16, ‘the statutes of Omri are kept’). So the witnesses reach a double agreement across their debate. On the number, the weight of judgment falls with Keil, Clarke, and Calmet — a scribe’s slip of one letter, not a contradiction to be forced — against Lightfoot’s ingenious but strained reckoning by the house of Omri. On the substance, all confess the darker fact the verse quietly names: a one-year king with Athaliah for his mother and Omri for his stock, the blood and the counsel of the north running in Judah’s veins. The short reign is no accident; the queen-mother’s ancestry tells the reader all he needs to know of how it will end.
27:
‘And he walked in the way of the house of Ahab, and did evil in the sight of the Lord, as did the house of Ahab: for he was the son in law of the house of Ahab.’ The witnesses hear in the thrice-repeated phrase the verse’s whole burden. The reformed John Gill names the twin idolatry of that house — Ahaziah went ‘worshipping the calves, and Baal also’ — joining the old schism-sin of Jeroboam to the imported cult of Jezebel. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott draws attention to the drumbeat of the language: he bids us ‘notice the threefold repetition of the words the house of Ahab,’ a deliberate hammering that fixes the source of the evil, and he cross-refers the Chronicler’s explanation that ‘his mother was his counsellor to do wickedly’ (2 Chronicles 22:3-4).

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch press the same point: Ahaziah ‘reigned in the spirit of Ahab, because he allowed his mother to act as his adviser.’ The evil is traced not to the young king’s own initiative so much as to the counsel he took — Athaliah at his ear, the house of Ahab at his back, the whole momentum of two corrupt dynasties carrying him where his father and grandfather had gone. The witnesses do not divide; they read the verse as the doctrine of evil influence written into a single sentence. A man is, the earlier commentators said, ‘according to the company he keeps and the copies he writes after,’ and Ahaziah copied Ahab because Ahab’s daughter had raised and now counselled him. The threefold ‘house of Ahab’ is thus no careless repetition but a verdict: son-in-law, disciple, and puppet of that house, he did its evil as surely as if he had been born inside its walls.

The commentators let the warning stand plain. The verse is a study in how sin propagates itself through kinship and counsel, how a marriage made a generation before determines the worship of a grandson, and how a mother’s advice can undo a nation. There is no debate here, only a shared and sober conclusion: Ahaziah did evil ‘as did the house of Ahab’ because he had become, in blood and in mind, a member of it. The chapter, which opened with a widow provided for by a prophet’s God, closes its royal survey with a king delivered over to his mother’s idols — the same providence that keeps David’s lamp burning also letting the house of Ahab draw Judah’s young king down into the ruin where, as the next chapter shows, it will itself be destroyed.
28:
‘And he went with Joram the son of Ahab to the war against Hazael king of Syria in Ramoth-gilead; and the Syrians wounded Joram.’ The reformed John Gill fixes the kinship — Joram was Ahaziah’s ‘mother’s brother, and so his uncle’ — and the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke the object of the campaign: the two kings went to ‘wrest Ramoth-gilead out of the hands of the Syrians,’ the very city where Ahab had been slain attempting the same (1 Kings 22:3).

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the alliance as the trap providence had set. The contest for Ramoth ‘had already cost Ahab his life’ and ‘was to furnish the occasion, according to the overruling providence of God, for the extermination of the whole of Omri’s family.’ Joram, wounded in the battle, ‘returned to Jezreel to be healed’; his nephew Ahaziah would visit him there, ‘and there he met with his death at the same time as Joram at the hands of Jehu’ (2 Kings 9:14; 2 Chronicles 22:7-9). Charles Ellicott defends the plain reading against Ewald, who would deny that Ahaziah shared the fighting: all the manuscripts and versions keep the preposition ‘with,’ the phrasing ‘wounded Joram’ and ‘king Joram went back’ assumes two kings in the field, and ‘the chronicler expressly states that Ahaziah accompanied Joram to Ramoth’ (2 Chronicles 22:5). He recalls, too, that Ahaziah went ‘by the persuasion of his mother and her family’ (2 Chronicles 22:4) — the same counsel that made him an idolater now marching him to his death.

The witnesses converge on the providential irony. Two kings of the accursed alliance — the son of Ahab and the son-in-law of Ahab’s house — ride out together against Hazael, the very man Elisha had wept to foresee, and the campaign that opens with a wound at Ramoth will close with a purge at Jezreel. The commentators see the hand of God threading it all: Ramoth-gilead, which drank Ahab’s blood, now draws his son and grandson into the same field, that the whole house might be gathered for judgment in one place and one hour. The alliance Jehoshaphat began in a marriage, and Athaliah maintained by her counsel, brings the last of the line to the battlefront exactly when Jehu is being anointed to destroy them. The witnesses do not divide; they read the verse as the tightening of a net — a wound that looks like the mischance of war but is, in truth, the appointed door through which the extermination of Omri’s house will come.
29:
‘And king Joram went back to be healed in Jezreel of the wounds which the Syrians had given him at Ramah… And Ahaziah… went down to see Joram the son of Ahab in Jezreel, because he was sick.’ The witnesses read the verse as the curtain rising on the tragedy of the next chapter. The reformed John Gill notes that Joram withdrew ‘from Ramoth, having taken it, and left his army there.’ The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch confirm that ‘Ramah’ is simply ‘Ramoth-gilead’ in short (compare 1 Kings 22:4).

Charles Ellicott supplies the topography and grammar. Joram ‘went back with a few personal attendants,’ leaving the army at Ramoth under its generals (2 Kings 9:14); he made for Jezreel, ‘the seat of the court at this time,’ because to reach Samaria he ‘would have had to cross a mountainous country, while he could be carried to Jezreel by an easier route through the valley of the Jordan.’ The imperfect verb ‘which the Syrians had given’ may imply ‘that Joram was wounded on more than one occasion.’ And Ellicott presses a point about the seam of the story: ‘now Ahaziah had gone down’ begins ‘a new paragraph,’ so that ‘the division of chapters is again at fault, there being no real break in the narrative between this verse and what follows.’

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reads the ending for its terrible providence. Joram ‘went back to be healed in Jezreel, and there he continued till Jehu conspired against and slew him there.’ So, Clarke writes, ‘the blood of the innocents, which had been shed by Ahab and his wife Jezebel, was visited on them in the total extinction of their family’ — the whole bloody sequel to be told at large in the chapters that follow. He adds his standing conviction that the chronological tangles of these histories are the fault of ‘copyists’ alone, to be cleared by ‘conjectural criticism’ rather than charged against the sacred writers. The witnesses converge, and the chapter ends by pointing beyond itself. A wounded king recovering at Jezreel, a nephew come down to visit him ‘because he was sick’ — the scene could not be more ordinary, and it is the very stage on which God will gather both kings for slaughter. Ellicott’s note that there is ‘no real break’ before the ninth chapter is more than an editor’s quibble: it is the reminder that the sickbed at Jezreel and the sword of Jehu belong to one act. The house of Ahab, the commentators agree, has walked, all unknowing, to the place of its execution; and the last words of the chapter — ‘because he was sick’ — are the innocent-seeming hinge on which the long-delayed judgment of that house will turn.
